q: Tangent and normal are drawn at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>16</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> on the parabola <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>16</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, which intersect the axis of the parabola at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math>, respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is the center of the circle through the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math><mi>∠</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>θ</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then a value of <math><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mrow></math> is:
question: Tangent and normal are drawn at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>16</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> on the parabola <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>16</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, which intersect the axis of the parabola at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math>, respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is the center of the circle through the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math><mi>∠</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>θ</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then a value of <math><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mrow></math> is:
solution:
Equation of tangent at x1,y1 is  yy1=2ax+x1

Equation of normal at x1,y1 is y-y1=-y12a(x-x1)

Here tangent and normal passes through P(16,16)

∴ Equation of tangent is 2y=x+16

Equation of normal is y+2x=48

Tangent and normal intersect x-axis at A-16,0 and B(24,0)

Since, APB is a right angled triangle, hence AB is the diameter for the circle.

Also mid point of AB is center of the circle.

Hence Centre =4,0

Slope of PC =43

Slope of PB=-2

tan⁡θ=43+21-2×43=10-5=2

subject: Mathematics
q: A tangent is drawn to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math> which is perpendicular to the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Which of the following points does NOT lie on it?
question: A tangent is drawn to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math> which is perpendicular to the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Which of the following points does NOT lie on it?
solution:
Slope of tangent =mT=m

So, m-2=-1⇒m=12

Equation: y=mx+am

⇒ y=12x+32×12   ∵a=64=32

⇒ y=x2+3

⇒ 2y=x+6

Point 5, 4 will not lie on it

q: Consider the parabola with vertex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> and the directrix <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> be the point where the parabola meets the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. If the normal to the parabola at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> intersects the parabola again at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>.</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>PQ</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to :
question: Consider the parabola with vertex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> and the directrix <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> be the point where the parabola meets the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. If the normal to the parabola at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> intersects the parabola again at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mo>.</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>PQ</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to :
solution:
Equation of the parabola is x-122=y-34   ...1

Put x=-12 in equation 1, we get y=74

Hence, point P is P-12,74

Now, differentiate equation 1 on both sides, we get y'=2x-12

At point P-12,74, the slope of tangent is -2.

Therefore, the slope of normal is 12

Equation of normal at P-12, 74 is x=2 y-4   ...2

On solving equations 1 & 2, we get the intersection point Q2, 3

∴(PQ)2=2+122+3-742

=254+2516=12516

q: A tangent line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is drawn at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> on the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is also tangent to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is equal to <u>                     </u> .
question: A tangent line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is drawn at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> on the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is also tangent to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is equal to <u>                     </u> .
solution:
Given, Parabola y2=8x  and circle x2+y2=a2

 Parabola y2=8x at point 2,-4

Differentiating w .r. t.  x''

2ydydx=8

dydx=4y

dydx2,-4=-1

Equation of tangent

y-y1=dydxx-x1

y+4=-1x-2

x+y+2=0

Line L is also tangent to the circle x2+y2=a2

So, OA=a   at 0,0

1×0+1×0+22=a

2=a

⇒a=2

If tangents are drawn from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> which meet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, then the area (in sq. units) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>S</mi><mi>O</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is equal to :
question: Let a parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be such that its vertex and focus lie on the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> units from the origin, respectively. If tangents are drawn from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> which meet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, then the area (in sq. units) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>S</mi><mi>O</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is equal to :
solution:
From the diagram, clearly RS is latus-rectum

∵ VF=4-22+0-02=2=a

∴ RS=4a=8

Now OF=2a=4

So, the area of triangle ORS=12×4×8=16

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> be a point on the parabola, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> , on the axis of the parabola. A line is now drawn through the mid-point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>N</mi></math>, parallel to its axis which meets the parabola at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. If the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>intercept of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NQ</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> be a point on the parabola, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> , on the axis of the parabola. A line is now drawn through the mid-point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>N</mi></math>, parallel to its axis which meets the parabola at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. If the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>intercept of the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NQ</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then :
solution:
Comparing the equation of the parabola, with standard equation y2=4ax, we get a=3.

Figure is drawn on the basis of the given information.

Parametric form of parabola is x=at2, y=2at

∴ P=3t2,6t, N=3t2,0, M=3t2,3t

⇒Q=3t24,3t

Equation of NQ:  y-0=3t-03t24-3t2x-3t2

i.e., y=-43t·x-3t2⇒y=-43t·x+4t

⇒4t=43⇒t=13

PN=6t=2

MQ=3t2-3t24=9t24=14

subject: Mathematics
q: Let one end of a focal chord of the parabola $y^{2}=16 x$ be $(16,16)$. If $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, \beta)$ divides this focal chord internally in the ratio $5: 2$, then the minimum value of $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to :
question: Let one end of a focal chord of the parabola $y^{2}=16 x$ be $(16,16)$. If $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, \beta)$ divides this focal chord internally in the ratio $5: 2$, then the minimum value of $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to :
solution:
Parabola $y^2 = 16x$, $a = 4$, focus at $(4, 0)$. Point $(16, 16)$ has parameter $t_1 = 2$.
Other end of focal chord: $t_2 = -1/t_1 = -1/2$, giving $(4 \cdot 1/4, 8 \cdot (-1/2)) = (1, -4)$.
P divides the chord in ratio 5:2 internally (two cases):
From $(16,16)$ to $(1,-4)$: $P = \left(\frac{37}{7}, \frac{12}{7}\right)$, $\alpha + \beta = 7$.
From $(1,-4)$ to $(16,16)$: $P = \left(\frac{82}{7}, \frac{72}{7}\right)$, $\alpha + \beta = 22$.
Minimum value of $\alpha + \beta = 7$.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the common tangent to the parabolas, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math> also touches the circle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is equal to :
question: If the common tangent to the parabolas, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math> also touches the circle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is equal to :
solution:
Given: y2=4x&x2=4y

Comparing with standard equations, y2=4a1x & x2=4a2y, a1=a2=1

Therefore tangent of y2=4x is y=mx+1m which is also tangent to x2=4y, whose equation would be y=mx-m2

∴  1m=-m2⇒  m=-1

∴ common tangent is y=-x-1, which touches x2+y2=c2

∴ (-1)2=c2(1+(-1)2)⇒ 1=c2·(1+1)⇒c2=12
